preamble: The complete elision of the velar stop [k] is the final stage of a lenition chain that typically moves from stop to fricative to glide before disappearing entirely.

examples.note: Finnish consonant gradation: strong grade /k/ â†’ weak grade âˆ…. E.g., 'puku' (suit) â†’ 'puvun' (GEN) â€” though that's kâ†’v. A cleaner kâ†’âˆ…: 'suku' â†’ 'suvun' shows kâ†’v; pure kâ†’âˆ… occurs e.g. 'jokea' â†’ 'jo-' in compounds.
Indonesian nasal prefix meN-: initial /k/ is deleted and replaced by the nasal /Å‹/ of the prefix (KPST rule: k, p, s, t deleted after nasal prefix).
Complete elision of the intervocalic velar stop.
Loss of the final velar consonant over time.

In Western Nuer dialects, word-final /k/ undergoes lenition to approximant [É°] or complete elision [âˆ…], distinguishing Western Nuer from the Jikany-based standard variety which retains /k/. This represents a synchronic or recent historical sound change marking dialect divergence within the Nuer speech community.

languageExamples.language: West Sogeram languages (Madang Province, Papua New Guinea)

examples.note: A striking innovation of the West Sogeram branch (Trans-New Guinea, Madang Province) is the complete loss of all word-initial consonants from polysyllabic words, regardless of place or manner of articulation. Proto-Sogeram *k- is deleted word-initially in polysyllabic contexts. This is documented by Don Daniels (2015, A Reconstruction of Proto-Sogeram) and represents an extreme consonant-initial weakening chain beginning with k-lenition.
